misconception: New oak and old oak produce the same effect on wine.
why_incorrect: New oak (first-fill barrique or barrel used for the first time) imparts significant flavour compounds — vanillin, lactones (coconut, sweet wood), toasty/spicy notes — and contributes tannins from the wood itself. It also allows a greater rate of micro-oxygenation. Old oak (second-fill or beyond) has had its extractable compounds largely depleted by previous use. It contributes minimal flavour or tannin and functions primarily as a vessel for controlled micro-oxygenation. Using 100% new oak vs. 100% old oak produces radically different wines from the same fruit. The percentage of new oak is a critical winemaking variable that skilled winemakers control precisely.
corrected_understanding: New oak adds significant flavour compounds (vanilla, spice, toast), wood tannins, and micro-oxygenation. Old oak is primarily a micro-oxygenation vessel with minimal flavour extraction. The distinction is fundamental to understanding oak's role in winemaking.
detection_signals: all oak barrels do the same thing
whether the barrel is new or old doesn't matter
